content.text: Major Incident Executive Review Process

Every SEV-1 incident requires more than a technical postmortem. It requires an executive review: a structured conversation with VP-level leadership that surfaces the business impact, secures the resources needed to prevent recurrence, and ensures that the organisation's reliability trajectory is visible to the people who set priorities. Without an executive review process, major incidents are invisible to leadership until they aggregate into a customer trust problem.
What the Executive Review Is Not

The executive review is not a blame session, a deep technical dive, or an extended postmortem meeting. Leadership does not need to understand the specifics of token budget management or embedding drift. They need to understand business impact, the gap between current and expected reliability, and what decisions they need to make.
Distinguish the executive review from the technical postmortem:
Dimension
Technical Postmortem
Executive Review
Audience
Engineering + SRE team
VP Engineering, CTO, Product VP, Customer Success VP
Depth
Full root cause analysis, contributing factors
Plain-language summary, no jargon
Focus
What broke and how to fix it technically
Business impact, resource needs, decisions required
Duration
60-90 minutes
30-45 minutes maximum
Cadence
Every SEV-1 and SEV-2
Every SEV-1; SEV-2 if customer-facing or safety-relevant
Output
Postmortem document + action items
Executive decisions + resource allocations
Trigger Criteria
Define explicit criteria that automatically trigger an executive review. This removes ambiguity about when the process applies:
Any SEV-1 incident
(full or near-total service unavailability, safety-relevant failure)
Any SEV-2 that affected
> 5% of paying customers for > 30 minutes
Any incident that generated
external press coverage or customer escalations to the CEO/board level
Any incident involving
a safety policy violation, regardless of severity classification
Any third SEV-2 incident in a calendar month
(volume signal requires leadership awareness)

The trigger is automatic. The incident commander does not decide whether an executive review is warranted — if the criteria are met, the review is scheduled, period. This prevents the common pattern where engineers underestimate the business significance of incidents and reviews never happen.
The Executive Brief

The executive brief is the most important output of the pre-review preparation. It is sent to all participants at least 12 hours before the review meeting. Executives read it before the meeting; the meeting itself assumes the brief has been read.
Brief Format (Maximum 2 Pages)

# Incident Executive Brief: [Incident ID] -- [Date]
Prepared by: [Incident Commander] + [Postmortem Owner]
Reviewed by: [Engineering Director]

---

## Impact Summary
- **Duration**: [start time] to [end time] ([N] hours [M] minutes)
- **Users affected**: [N] (estimated [X%] of active users)
- **Services down**: [service names in plain language]
- **Customer-facing**: Yes / No
- **Safety-relevant**: Yes / No
- **SLA breach**: Yes / No ([customer name] SLA requires 99.9% monthly)

## What Happened (Plain Language)
[2-3 sentences. No jargon. Write as if explaining to a non-technical board member.]

Example: "Our AI recommendation system stopped responding for 47 minutes
because a software update reduced the memory available to the service below
the minimum needed to run. Customers saw error messages instead of
recommendations during this window."

## Timeline
- [HH:MM] Failure began
- [HH:MM] First alert fired (MTTD: [N] minutes)
- [HH:MM] On-call engineer engaged (MTTA: [N] minutes)
- [HH:MM] Service restored (MTTR: [N] minutes)
- [HH:MM] Full investigation completed

## Root Cause
[1-2 sentences. Plain language. Avoid technical acronyms.]

## Top 3 Action Items
| Item | Owner | Due | Decision Needed from Leadership |
|------|-------|-----|--------------------------------|
| [short title] | [name] | [date] | [Yes/No: what do you need?] |

## What Went Well
- [bullet]
- [bullet]

## Customer Communications Status
- Status page: [updated / not updated]
- Direct customer outreach: [sent / pending / not required]
- Enterprise customers notified: [Yes / No]

The 'Decision Needed from Leadership' column in the action items table is the most critical element. It converts the review from a status update into a decision meeting. If no item requires a leadership decision, the review can be async.
The Review Meeting
Attendees
Required:
Incident Commander (or designate)
— presents the brief, answers technical questions in plain language
VP Engineering
— owns reliability investments and priority tradeoffs
VP Product
— owns customer commitments and feature-reliability balance
Optional based on incident type:
CTO
— for safety-relevant incidents or board-level customer escalations
VP Customer Success
— when specific enterprise customers were significantly affected
General Counsel
— if the incident has regulatory, contractual, or legal implications
Not included: individual contributors, unless they are presenting a specific analysis. The meeting is a decision forum, not a debrief audience.
Structured Agenda
The agenda is fixed and enforced. No item is skipped; no section runs over its time allocation without unanimous agreement to extend.
0-5 min: Impact summary
— IC presents the key numbers (duration, users affected, SLA status). Assumes brief has been read.
5-15 min: Timeline and root cause
— walkthrough of the timeline. Root cause in plain language. One follow-up question per attendee maximum.
15-25 min: Action items requiring decisions
— the IC presents each action item that needs a leadership decision. Decisions are made in the meeting, not deferred.
25-35 min: Resource and priority discussion
— if systemic fixes require additional engineering capacity or de-prioritisation of feature work, this is where the tradeoff is made explicit.
35-40 min: Customer and external communications
— status page update status, enterprise customer outreach, any regulatory notification requirements.
40-45 min: Confirm decisions
— the IC reads back every decision made in the meeting, with the named owner and due date. No one leaves unclear about what was decided.
The Decision Log
The most important output of the review meeting is the decision log. It is written in real time by a designated note-taker (not the IC) and distributed within 2 hours of the meeting:

# Executive Review Decision Log
Incident: [ID] | Meeting date: [date] | Attendees: [names]

## Decisions Made
1. **[decision]**
   Owner: [name] | Due: [date] | Follow-up: [when/how]

2. **[decision]**
   Owner: [name] | Due: [date] | Follow-up: [when/how]

## Resource Allocations
- [engineer name] re-allocated from [project] to [reliability work] through [date]
- [infrastructure investment] approved: [amount / resources]

## Deferred Items
- [item] deferred to quarterly planning; IC to re-raise in [month] review

## Next Check-in
- [date/format]: [who reviews progress on which decisions]
Handling Safety-Relevant Major Incidents
Executive reviews for safety-relevant incidents follow a modified process:
AI Safety team lead
is a required attendee, not optional
The review is scheduled within
24 hours rather than 48
The brief includes a
'threat model update' section
: what variants of this failure pattern were not previously in the evaluation suite?
The meeting produces an explicit
deployment gate decision
: is the affected system allowed to serve production traffic until the safety action items are closed?
A follow-up review is
automatically scheduled for 30 days out
to verify closure of safety action items

For safety incidents, the executive review is not just a process checkpoint — it is the mechanism by which leadership formally accepts or rejects the residual risk of continuing to operate the service while fixes are in progress. This decision must be explicit and documented, not implicit.
Common Failure Modes in Executive Reviews
Failure mode
Symptom
Fix
No brief sent in advance
Meeting becomes a status update instead of a decision forum
Enforce 12h pre-read requirement; reschedule if brief is late
Too technical
Leadership disengages; no useful decisions are made
IC prepares plain-language translation; engineer reviews brief for jargon before sending
No decisions required
Review is a one-way information session; could be async
Cancel the meeting; send the brief async with a 48h response window
Blame framing
Engineers withhold information; blameless culture erodes
Facilitator reframes any blame language as 'system factors'
Action items not followed up
Review becomes a ritual with no impact on reliability
Decisions from the log are tracked weekly alongside postmortem action items
Preparing the Incident Commander for the Review
The IC is typically an engineer who has just managed a high-stress incident. Presenting to VP-level leadership 24-48 hours later is a different skill set. Prepare the IC with a structured rehearsal:
Pre-Meeting Rehearsal (30 minutes)
IC presents the brief verbally to the Engineering Director or a senior peer. Time it.
The reviewer asks the questions most likely to come from leadership (see below) and the IC practices plain-language answers.
The reviewer flags any technical jargon in the brief and the IC rewrites those sentences.
The IC and reviewer align on what decisions they will ask leadership to make — and what happens if leadership says no to each request.
Likely Leadership Questions and Prepared Answers
Prepare concise answers to these before the meeting:
Leadership question
What they are really asking
Prepared answer shape
"Could this happen again?"
Is my trust in the platform justified right now?
Yes/No + what specifically prevents recurrence + when
"Why didn't we detect it faster?"
Is our monitoring investment adequate?
Root cause of detection gap + what investment closes it
"What did we tell customers?"
Have we managed the external trust damage?
Status page timeline + direct outreach status + messaging summary
"How much did this cost us?"
Should I be concerned about contractual or financial exposure?
Estimated user-hours lost + SLA breach status + known enterprise concerns
"Is this a pattern?"
Should I be escalating reliability to a board-level concern?
Prior occurrences + recurrence rate trend + trajectory

The IC does not need to know everything. 'I don't know, but I'll find out and follow up by [date]' is a complete answer for questions outside the IC's knowledge. What the IC should never do is guess or speculate in front of leadership — incorrect information in an executive setting is harder to correct than acknowledged uncertainty.
Async Review Option

Not every major incident requires a synchronous meeting. If the executive brief has no decision items — all action items are already resourced and owned, no cross-team escalations are needed, and no enterprise customers require executive outreach — the review can be conducted asynchronously:

Async Review Process (for SEV-1 incidents with no pending exec decisions)

1. Brief is sent to VP distribution list with subject:
   "[ASYNC REVIEW] Incident [ID] Brief -- Response by [date+48h]"

2. Each executive responds in writing to exactly two questions:
   a) Any concerns or questions requiring follow-up?
   b) Any resource or priority decisions needed from you?

3. If no responses raise concerns within 48h, the review is complete.
   The IC notes "async review completed" in the postmortem.

4. If any response raises a concern, a synchronous meeting is scheduled
   within the next 48h. The original async thread becomes the pre-read.

The async option reduces meeting load without eliminating leadership visibility. Teams that use it effectively find that roughly 40% of SEV-1 reviews can be handled async, preserving synchronous meetings for incidents where decisions genuinely require real-time discussion.
Integration with On-Call Rotation Health
Major incident reviews reveal patterns about on-call health that no other process surfaces. Track across reviews:
Which engineers have been IC more than twice in a quarter?
High IC concentration indicates rota imbalance or insufficient IC training in the wider team.
What percentage of incidents escalated past the primary on-call?
High escalation rates indicate either alert misconfiguration (wrong person being paged) or skill gaps.
What was the time-of-day distribution of incidents?
Off-hours concentration suggests insufficient monitoring automation and over-reliance on human response.

Present these patterns in the quarterly aggregate review alongside the incident volume and MTTx metrics. On-call health is a leading indicator of engineer burnout, which is itself a reliability risk: burned-out engineers make mistakes and eventually leave, degrading both the team's incident response capability and its institutional knowledge.
The Aggregate Review: Quarter-End Major Incident Summary
In addition to per-incident reviews, run a quarterly aggregate review for leadership that covers all major incidents from the prior quarter:
Total SEV-1 count vs prior quarter and year-over-year
Cumulative user-impact hours (sum of duration x affected users across all SEV-1s)
Reliability investment made this quarter vs plan
Action items from prior quarter reviews: how many closed on time?
Top three systemic root causes that drove the most impact
The quarterly aggregate review is the mechanism by which reliability becomes a standing agenda item at the VP level, rather than something that only surfaces during crises.
Summary

The major incident executive review converts a significant operational failure into a leadership decision point. Its value is not in surfacing technical details — the postmortem does that — but in ensuring that the people who control engineering priorities and resource allocation understand the business impact of reliability failures and have a structured forum to make decisions about prevention investments. The core components are clear trigger criteria, a pre-read brief (maximum two pages, plain language), a structured 45-minute meeting that produces a decision log, and a follow-up cadence for those decisions. Safety-relevant incidents require a modified process with mandatory AI safety team participation and explicit deployment gate decisions.
Key takeaways:
Trigger criteria are automatic -- IC does not decide; the criteria decide
The brief goes out 12h before the meeting; the meeting assumes it has been read
Every action item in the brief should answer: what decision does leadership need to make?
The decision log is distributed within 2 hours; decisions are tracked weekly
Safety incidents need an explicit deployment gate decision, not just action items
